prompt_md:
Your team processes **10 million** address strings a month to standardize them (fix casing, expand abbreviations, flag undeliverable ones). About **95%** follow well-known postal patterns; the rest are messy free-form text a rule cannot parse. A teammate proposes sending **every** address to an LLM for consistency. Cost and latency matter at this volume, and standardized addresses must be **exact and repeatable**.

Which design best fits — and why?

decision_choices: Send all 10M addresses to the LLM every month for one consistent approach
Use deterministic rules for everything and drop the messy 5% as undeliverable
Deterministic parsing first for the ~95% that match known patterns; fall back to the LLM only for the messy remainder, then validate every result against postal rules
Fine-tune a model on past addresses and route 100% of traffic through it
reason_choices: Because a deterministic-first design keeps most traffic on the cheap, exact, instant path and reserves AI for the fraction that genuinely needs it — and validating the AI output keeps standardized addresses exact and repeatable
Because calling AI on every input is the most consistent and therefore cheapest option at scale
Because messy inputs have no value and are safe to discard
Because routing everything through one model removes the need for any deterministic validation
